coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots: Comprehensively understanding of the internal quenching mechanism and application for catechol detection
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Lei
Wen, Jiaxin
Li, Kunjian
Liu, Lu
Wang, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new member of the nanocarbon family, are emerging as attractive optical sensing material. At present, there is still a lack of in-depth investigation on internal responsive mechanism of CQDs as their fluorescence is quenched by analytes. In this work, ethylenediamine (EDA) functionalized carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) are synthesized through the protection/deprotection of one amine group and the amidation reaction on CQDs surface. According to the fluorescence response of N-CQDs in catechol (CTC) solution and under different environmental conditions, it is found that the EDA on the surface of N-CQDs could spontaneously combine with CTC via Michael addition or Schiff base reaction to form static compounds, which serve as electron donor to effectively quench the fluorescence originated from sp2 carbogenic core area and “surface state” of N-CQDs, but have little impact on the emission from “edge state” of N-CQDs. This is the first time that the “inertness” of “edge state” was observed. Based on the comprehensive analysis of quenching mechanism, the excitation wavelength (λex=310nm) corresponding to the “core state” of N-CQDs was selected, and a simple, highly sensitive and selective detection platform for CTC quantification is established.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Quenching mechanism
Catechol detection
Ethylenediamine
core state

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of N-CQDs and CQDs to pH
Fluorescence response of CQDs and N-CQDs to CTC and its isomers
Application of N-CQDs for CTC detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Major Science and Technology Program for Water Pollution Control and Treatment
National Key Research and Development Program of China
Natural Science Foundation of Tianjin City

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis route of N-CQDs and detection for CTC.

(a) XRD patterns of CQDs and N-CQDs, (b) TEM image and actual fluorescence picture (excitation wavelength: 365 nm) of synthesized CQDs, (c) TEM image and actual fluorescence picture (excitation wavelength: 365 nm) of synthesized N-CQDs, and (d) FTIR spectra of CQDs and N-CQDs.
(a) UV–vis absorbance spectra of CQDs and N-CQDs, and (b) three-dimensional fluorescent matric scanning map of N-CQDs (excitation wavelength: 200-500 nm; emission wavelength: 220-700 nm).
Relative variation of fluorescence intensity at pH 6 and 10 under different excitation wavelengths (F6: fluorescence intensity at pH 6, F10: fluorescence intensity at pH 10).
Schematic diagram of combination mechanism between CTC and N-CQDs.

Normalized fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs (0.02 mg mL−1) with the addition of CTC, RS and HQ at the excitation wavelength of (a) 310 nm, (b) 370 nm, (c) 460 nm in PBS buffer (0.01 M, pH = 7.4, 0.5 h), and (d) corresponding N-CQDs fluorescence quenching rate of CTC, RS and HQ system. Quenching rate=(F 0-F 100)/F 0×100 %, where F 0: fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs in the absence of analyte, F 100: fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs mixed with analyte (100 μM).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs with various concentrations of CTC (excitation wavelength: 310 nm), (b) linear relationship between (F 0-F)/F and CTC concentrations, F 0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs before and after the addition of various concentrations of CTC.
Fluorescence lifetimes of N-CQDs (0.02 mg mL-1) before and after the addition of CTC (100 μM).
Schematic diagram of fluorescence quenching mechanism on the surface of N-CQDs.
Schematic diagram of fluorescence quenching mechanism inside of N-CQDs.

Fluorescence quenching rate of N-CQDs in the presence of (a) different organics and (b) ions (Organics concentrations are 50 μM except oxytetracycline is 10 μM, EDTA is 20 μM, ions concentrations are 10 μM).

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots: Comprehensively understanding of the internal quenching mechanism and application for catechol detection

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Nankai University, Tianjin, 300350, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new member of the nanocarbon family, are emerging as attractive optical sensing material. At present, there is still a lack of in-depth investigation on internal responsive mechanism of CQDs as their fluorescence is quenched by analytes. In this work, ethylenediamine (EDA) functionalized carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) are synthesized through the protection/deprotection of one amine group and the amidation reaction on CQDs surface. According to the fluorescence response of N-CQDs in catechol (CTC) solution and under different environmental conditions, it is found that the EDA on the surface of N-CQDs could spontaneously combine with CTC via Michael addition or Schiff base reaction to form static compounds, which serve as electron donor to effectively quench the fluorescence originated from sp2 carbogenic core area and “surface state” of N-CQDs, but have little impact on the emission from “edge state” of N-CQDs. This is the first time that the “inertness” of “edge state” was observed. Based on the comprehensive analysis of quenching mechanism, the excitation wavelength (λex=310nm) corresponding to the “core state” of N-CQDs was selected, and a simple, highly sensitive and selective detection platform for CTC quantification is established.

ce:section.ce:para: Catechol (1.2-dihydroxybenze, CTC) plays an important part in chemical industry as organic raw material for production of pharmaceuticals, light stabilizers, pesticides, antioxidant and dyes [1–3]. This compound is easily released to the environment during its manufacture and applications, causing serious damage to ecological environment and human health even at a low concentration [4]. Moreover, CTC frequently coexists with its isomers such as resorcinol (1,3-dihydroxybenze, RS) and hydroquinone (1,4-dihydroxybenze, HQ) in environmental samples [5]. Thus, it is urgent to develop a selective and reliable method to distinguish and determine it. Up to now, many analytical methods for determination of CTC have been established, including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6], spectrophotometry [7], gas chromatography-mass spectrometry [8], chemiluminescence [9], electrochemical methods [10,11]. Most of these methods are high cost or require time-consuming pre-treatment processes. Therefore, it is still necessary to develop rapid and convenient analytical approaches for CTC determination.

The fluorescent method has attracted more attentions in the field of environmental detection due to its high sensitivity, simplicity and relatively inexpensive instruments [12]. Compared to the other inorganic or organic fluorescent probes such as CdTe quantum dots, Fura Red, Calcium Crimson and so on [13–16], carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are becoming a research focus because of their strong and stable photoluminescence, chemical inertness, biocompatibility and low toxicity [17–19]. Based on these excellent performances, CQDs have been extensively reported in applications of sensing various metal ions and organic molecules according to the change of fluorescence intensity [20–22].

Currently, most of the research on the CQDs-sensing platform selects the maximum excitation wavelength (around 360 nm) or maximum emission wavelength as the detection wavelength [20], [23,24], and the detection mechanisms analyses basically focus on the fluorescence behavior occurring on the surface of CQDs or in the external environment [23–26]. Ding et al. reported their prepared CQDs could determine rutin and Al3+ through electrostatic attractions on CQDs surface [24]. Yu et al. developed nitrogen-doped CQDs for humic substance detection, and proved the fluorescence quenching was caused by a nonradiative electron transfer from CQDs to humic substance [25]. It is notable that CQDs have complex fluorescence origins, such as the sp2 carbon core embedded within sp3 matrix(“core state”), the zig-zag structure at the edge of graphite core(“edge state”) and various surface detect or fluorophore molecules on the external surface of CQDs(“surface state”) [27–33]. The different fluorescence origins of CQDs have their own properties. Therefore, the change of fluorescence signal induced by external quenching agents might be closely related to different internal structures of CQDs [27–33]. However, these intrinsic mechanisms have been little investigated. If the relationships between the fluorescence signal change caused by extrinsic quenching agents and the corresponding intrinsic emission sites can be clarified, it will help researchers find the optimistic detection conditions and deeply understand the associated detection mechanism. To establish the connection between internal emission sites and external analytes, it is necessary to induce some specific molecules or groups onto the surface of CQDs as media.

In this work, the carboxylic group on the surface of CQDs was chemically modified by introducing ethylenediamine (EDA) via an amidation reaction considering the potential reaction between EDA and CTC, and one end of EDA was kept free through protection and deprotection during the modification process. The EDA linked CQDs (N-CQDs) mainly have three emission origins (“core state”, “edge state” and “surface state”). The EDA on the surface of N-CQDs could rapidly react with CTC to form static compounds. According to the evaluation on fluorescent response of N-CQDs to different pH, excitation wavelengths and external analytes(CTC and its isomers), the fluorescences from “core state” and “surface state” show almost consistent response behavior, while the “edge state” fluorescence keeps exclusive “inertness” to analytes and pH change. The fluorescence quenching mechanism corresponding to different structures of N-CQDs (from the outer surface to inner core) is comprehensively elaborated. Based on the investigation of detection range, detection limit and selectivity of N-CQDs to CTC, we established a simple, highly sensitive and selective detection platform for CTC quantification. Moreover, the proposed platform shows good feasibility and practicability for CTC determination in environmental water samples.

Citric acid, urea, ethylene glycol, EDA, N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-boc-ethylenediamine (Boc-EDA), EDTA, vitamin C, benzoic acid, p-aminophenol, CTC, RS, HQ, phenol, sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), Disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4), Dipotassium hydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4), barium chloride (BaCl2), calcium chloride (CaCl2), potassium dichromate (K2CrO7), ferric chloride (FeCl3) and other mineral salts were purchased from Sanjiang Chemical Technology Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) was purchased from Energy Chemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China), and 2-(N-Morpholino)-ethanesulfonic acid (MES) and oxytetracycline were purchased from Dingguo Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Tryptophan and acetamiprid were purchased from Solomon Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Ultrapure water obtained from a Milli-Q water purification system was used throughout the experiments. All the reagents were analytical grade and received without further purification.

The structure and crystallinity of prepared materials were determined by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) (Ultima IV X-ray diffractometer, Rigaku, Japan) under the following conditions: 40 kV, 40 mA, 2θ range of 10−80° with CuKa radiation. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded by JEM-2800 microscopy (JEOl, Japan) at 200 kV using ultrathin carbon film coated copper grid as substrates. The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) was measured on a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi electron spectrometer with a monochromatic Al Ka X-ray source. The C1s binding energy was corrected at 284.6 eV. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on Tensor-37 FTIR spectrophotometer (Bruker, Germany) within a range of 400-4000 cm−1. The fluorescence spectra and three-dimensional fluorescent matric scanning map were performed on F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) with excitation and emission slit set at 2.5 nm and 5.0 nm respectively, and the PMT voltage was set at 700 V. UV/Vis absorption spectra were measured using Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). The time-resolved decay curves were obtained on FLS980 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh, UK).

CQDs were prepared via a bottom-up method reported in our previous work [34]. Typically, 0.615 g citric acid and 0.385 g urea were dissolved into 30 mL ethyleneglycol. After stirring for 2 h, the mixture was placed into 50 mL teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 7 h. The black product was cooled to room temperature and then filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove the large particle impurities. Subsequently, the product was purified by dialyzing against distilled water with a dialysis membrane (1000 MWCO) for 48 h, and the outer distilled water was changed three times a day. Then, the CQDs powder was obtained through vacuum freeze-drying. A certain amount of CQDs powder was dissolved into distilled water to obtained the standard solution of CQDs with the concentration of 1 mg/mL, which was stored in 4 °C for further application.

To prepare the N-CQDs, CQDs (25 mg) powder was dispersed in MES (25 mL) buffer liquid (pH = 6) though ultrasonic vibration for 30 min. Then the mixture was deaerated with nitrogen before adding EDC (100 mg) and NHS (50 mg). Subsequently, the solution was incubated in ice-bath and nitrogen atmosphere for 12 h under magnetic stirring. Next, Boc-EDA (200 μL) was dropped into the above solution and the pH value was adjusted to 7.4 by addition of NaOH (1 M, 2 mL) dropwise. After constantly stirring for 24 h under nitrogen atmosphere and ice-bath condition, CQDs with Boc-EDA molecules connecting onto their surface were basically formed. Many bubbles appeared after adding HCl (10 mL, 1 M) in the above mixture and continuously stirring for 3 h at room temperature, which might be ascribed to the transformation from Boc-EDA to EDA on the surface of CQDs. Afterwards the pH value was adjusted to around 6 by adding NaHCO3 and the solution was dialyzed against distilled water (1500 mL) for 48 h to remove impurities. Finally, N-CQDs powder were obtained after vacuum freeze-drying. A certain amount of N-CQDs powder was dispersed into distilled water to obtain the standard solution of N-CQDs (1 mg mL−1), which was stored in 4 °C for further experiments (Scheme 1 ).

N-CQDs (1 mL, 0.2 g L−1) suspension was pipetted into a vial (10 mL) and diluted with PBS buffer solution (8 mL, 0.01 M, pH = 7.4) or NaHCO3-NaOH buffer solution (0.025 M, pH = 9.8), and then CTC solution (1 mL) were added. The fluorescence of mixture was measured at room temperature after 30 min. The same processes were used to investigate the interferences of HQ, RS and other substances (phenol, acetamiprid, benzoic acid, p-aminophenol, oxytetracycline, tryptophan, lysine, tyrosine and various ions).

Quinine sulfate was used as a standard (Φ0 = 0.54). In order to minimize the reabsorption effects, the absorbance was kept below 0.05. The quantum yield of CQDs/N-CQDs (Φa) was calculated according to the following equation:
Φ a
=
Φ 0
F a
A 0
n a 2
F 0
A a
n 0 2
Where Fa and F0 is the integrated area in emission spectra of CQDs/N-CQDs and quinine sulfate, respectively. Aa and A0 is the absorbance of CQDs/N-CQDs and quinine sulfate, respectively. na and n0 is the refractive indice of CQDs/N-CQDs (dissolved in distilled water) and quinine sulfate (dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4), respectively.

As illustrated in Fig. 1 a, the XRD profiles of CQDs and N-CQDs show a broad peak centered at around 26.5° (similar to the (002) planes of graphite) [35,36], which can be attributed to highly disordered carbon atoms. According to TEM images (Fig. 1b and c), the as-prepared CQDs and N-CQDs are both spherical particles with diameter of 27.63 ± 6.04 nm and 26.83 ± 6.43 nm respectively (Figure S1), suggesting no evident size change of CQDs after being modified. Figure S2 exhibits the SEM and HR-TEM images of N-CQDs.

The FTIR spectra of CQDs and N-CQDs were performed to characterize the surface functional groups (Fig. 1d). The peaks at about 3375 cm−1 and 3232 cm−1 correspond to stretching vibrations of OH and NH, respectively [37]. The peak centered at 2966 cm−1 is attributed to CH unsaturated stretching vibration [38]. Besides, the strong signal at 1695 cm−1 is assigned to vibrational transition of CO [39]. The stretching vibration of CC is detected at 1601 cm−1, and the absorption at 1410 cm−1 results from the stretching of CN [40,41]. These results demonstrate that hydrophilic groups extensively exist on the surface of CQDs and N-CQDs, which increases the water solubility and stability of these materials.

EDA molecule has some chemical groups (like CN, NH) similar to CQDs, thus XPS was employed to further distinguish the groups on the surface of CQDs and N-CQDs (Figure S3). C1s (285.49 eV/285.25 eV), O1s (531.96 eV/532.04 eV) and N1s (400.05 eV/400.04 eV) peaks are all observed in both samples [42]. The high resolution C1s XPS spectrum of N-CQDs indicates the presence of CO (288.4 eV), CN/CO (286.03 eV) and CC/CC (284.76 eV) [43]. The N1s spectrum of N-CQDs is composed of two peaks centered at 399.71 eV(N(C)3/N(C)2) and 400.29 eV (amino N) [44]. O1s peak of N-CQDs can be resolved into three peaks at 533.11 eV, 532.12 eV and 531.17 eV, corresponding to OCO, CO and CO, respectively [45]. Compared with CQDs, the relative content of CN/CO in N-CQDs increases from 8.43 % to 11.92 %, the amino N proportion rises from 2.35 % to 3.44 %, while the CO content decreases from 12.84 % to 5.4 %. These changes probably resulted from the combination of EDA molecules onto the surface of CQDs through the amidation reaction between carboxyl groups of CQDs and EDA.

The optical properties of N-CQDs and CQDs were determined by UV–vis and fluorescence spectrometers (Fig. 2 a). The absorption bands appear between 230 nm and 310 nm were related to the π-π* transition in intrinsic sp2-hybridized carbon clusters (core state).27, 46 The broaden absorption shoulders centered at 370 nm were assigned to n–π* transition of chemical bonds at the edge of graphitized carbon core (i.e., CO) (edge state), which are commonly the main emission sites responsible for bright fluorescence of CQDs [20,27,47], In addition, the weaker absorption bands centered at around 450 nm with extended tails may result from the fluorescent chromophores attached to the edge of carbon core (surface state) [29,32,46,47]. It is noticeable the absorption bands originated from the surface state transition overlap with the absorption bands originated from the edge state transition without a clear border, which might be attributed to the close contact between the two states.

Fluorescence emissions of N-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths are shown in Figure S4. It is obvious that the emission spectra can be divided into three categories. When λex (excitation wavelength) is less than 310 nm, the emission peaks position remains at around 500 nm and the intensity declines continually as the increase of λex. The N-CQDs show an excitation-independence performance at λex of 320 nm–400 nm, and the emission peaks blueshift to around 460 nm. The emission intensity firstly increases and then significantly decreases above the excitation wavelength of 380 nm. Further increasing excitation wavelength from 410 nm to 480 nm, the emission peak gradually redshifts accompanied by a considerably decrease of intensity. These results are similar to those reported in the literature [29].

The fluorescence quantum yield of CQDs and N-CQDs is 4.59 % and 9.47 %, respectively. To investigate the fluorescence behavior of as-prepared materials more intuitively, the three-dimensional fluorescent matric scanning was performed. As shown in Figs. 2b and S3d, three clear fluorescence areas have corresponding excitation center at 270 nm, 370 nm and 450 nm, which is called as Zone A, Zone B and Zone C, respectively. It is evident that the λex ranges of the three areas match well with the results of UV-vis absorption spectra. Taking the above two-dimensional fluorescence spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra into account, the origin of the three emission regions is probably assigned to the intrinsic “carbon core” (π-π* transition), “edge state” (n-π* transition) and “surface state” (surface defects or fluorophores), respectively [27,47].

The influence of salt addition on the stability of CQDs and N-CQDs is shown in the Figure S5. The fluorescence intensity remained basically stable when the concentration of NaCl was increased from 10−9 to 1 M, which shows that the CQDs and N-CQDs were optically stable and there was no obvious ionization of surface groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of N-CQDs and CQDs to pH
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs and CQDs has been measured under a wide range of pH value (1∼12). As illustrated in Figure S6 and S7, the fluorescence intensity of two materials at λex 280 nm, 310 nm and 460 nm remains at low level when pH value is below 6, and then soars to the maximum at pH 10. The remarkable change of fluorescence intensity possibly originated from extensive protonation/deprotonation of the surface amide groups and/or carboxyl groups [30]. Interestingly, the different trend can be observed when the excitation wavelength is 370 nm. The fluorescence intensity of CQDS increases with the increase of pH under acidic conditions (pH 1–4), and this change slows down obviously above pH 4. While the fluorescence of N-CQDs owing less surface −COOH and more surface –NH2 only increases slightly in the whole range of pH 1−14. The fluorescence of N-CQDs and CQDs is both insensitive to pH variations in the range of 6−10. These results indicate that “core state” and “surface state” show a similar pH dependence, while the “edge state” has a distinctive fluorescence response performance. This might be explained by the fact that the “edge state” is isolated from the external solution by the outermost “surface state”, which leads to its “inertness”. The similar performance of “core state” and “surface state” means there is possibly some inherent connections between them.

The relative variation of fluorescence intensity ((F 10-F 6)/F 6) at pH 6 and 10 was adopted to characterize the pH response distinction between CQDs and N-CQDs (Fig. 3 ). Compared with CQDs, N-CQDs experience more remarkable increase of fluorescence intensity at all the selected λex except for λex 370 nm, which exhibits N-CQDs are more sensitive to the solution pH change. During the modification process, −COOH was consumed through the reaction with EDA, and more –NH2 was induced onto the CQDs surface. The –NH2 on one side of EDA molecule was specially protected by t-Butyloxy carbonyl groups (Boc) during the process of modification, and then released after the –NH2 on the other side of EDA was bound onto the surface of CQDs. The free –NH2 of EDA on N-CQDs might play an essential part in fluorescence emitted from sp2 carbon core and “surface state”. Different from the previously reported effect of −COOH on the fluorescence of CQDs [29], H+ can easily be attached on nitrogen atom of -NH2 in acid solution in this work, which would increase the ability to trap excited electrons and hinder the carriers recombination. In alkaline solution, H+ is easily withdrawn from -NH2 due to deprotonation and then the fluorescence is recovered. It is worth noting that the EDA on the surface of N-CQDs has not significantly improved the pH sensitivity of fluorescence relating to the “edge state” in the neutral and alkaline range (pH 6–10).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of CQDs and N-CQDs to CTC and its isomers
ce:section.ce:para: In order to understand the interaction between N-CQDs and analytes, the potential reactivity of EDA with CTC and its isomers was firstly evaluated. The fluorescence emission spectra of CTC, HQ and RS alone were measured (Figure S8a, b and c), and their self-fluorescence interferences could be excluded. Then, the fluorescence spectra of the mixture of EDA and CTC or its isomers were recorded after reacting for 0.5 h at room temperature. Figure S8d, e and f reveal that stronger fluorescence emission appears and the fluorescence intensity increases obviously with the increase of analytes concentration only in EDA-CTC mixture system, indicating there might be some new compounds produced owing to the combination of EDA and CTC. Correspondingly, two bright emission regions can be observed in the three-dimension fluorescence map of EDA-CTC mixture (Figure S9). There are only considerably weak emissions in maps of EDA-HQ and EDA-RS mixtures. As shown in absorption spectra of Figure S10, several new bands formed in all the three mixture systems, and CTC-EDA mixture shows maximum absorption value in the whole wavelength range, implying CTC has the strongest reaction capacity with EDA among its isomers.

The formation of catechol-amino polymers between the CTC and compounds containing amino groups (e.g. dopamine, 1,6-hexamethylenediamine, triethylenetetramine etc.) has been reported in the field of biomolecule immobilization [48–50]. CTC is easily oxidized into 1,2-benzoquinone by air especially under alkaline condition [48], and can combine with amino groups through Schiff base or Michael addition reaction. The possible interactions between CTC and EDA are shown in Figure S11. In the Michael addition reaction, some compounds analogous to dopamine molecule might be formed.

Based on the above analysis, similar reactions may also occur between CTC and EDA bound on surface of N-CQDs through a combination of CTC and free NH2. Therefore, the influence of CTC on the absorption spectrum of N-CQDs was examined. CTC has a sharp characteristic peak at 287.5 nm (Figure S12). This absorption peak slightly blue-shifted to 273.5 nm when CTC was mixed with N-CQDs, verifying the static combination of CTC and N-CQDs. Additionally, Boc-EDA, owning NH2 only on one terminal of EDA, similar to EDA connected on N-CQDs, was also used to investigate its influence on absorption intensity of CTC (Figure S13). There was a linear relationship between concentration and absorbance of CTC. The different slopes of regression line in CTC and CTC-Boc-EDA systems further identify the chemical adherence occurred on surface of N-CQDs originates from interactions between CTC and EDA. The associated reaction ways between CTC and N-CQDs are shown in Fig. 4 .

The fluorescence response of prepared materials to CTC and its isomers was evaluated comprehensively. As illustrated in Fig. 5 , the fluorescence of N-CQDs was quenched by CTC with a corresponding quenching rate of 66.51 % and 57.76 % respectively when excited at 310 nm (from Zone A) and 460 nm (from Zone C) at pH 7.4, while HQ and RS have minor effect on fluorescence signal of N-CQDs. However, there is neglectable change in N-CQDs fluorescence intensity of all the three systems at the excitation wavelength of 370 nm (from Zone B). When pH value was adjusted to 9.8, similar fluorescence intensity change tendencies can be observed in the three systems (Figure S14), and these changes become more irregular, which might be attributed to the increase of charge distribution complexity on the surface of N-CQDs due to a high degree of deprotonation. In addition, the fluorescence emitted by “core state” and “surface state” presents similar quenching behavior, while the “edge state” fluorescence still shows an insensitivity in CTC system, which is consistent with the response tendency of three emission states at different pH value.

Compared to pH 7.4 system, the fluorescence quenching efficiency by CTC has dropped to 51.26 % and 34.16 % when excited at 310 nm and 460 nm respectively in pH 9.8 system. A previous study revealed that CTC and oxidized CTC (quinone state material) are in equilibrium in aqueous media, and the equilibrium shifts toward the higher concentration of quinone state material at alkaline pH. [51] Thus, we proposed that both the CTC bound with EDA and its oxidized state material have quenching effect on N-CQDs, but their quenching abilities are different. In comparison with quinone state material, the bonded CTC may have stronger quenching ability. A confirmatory experiment was conducted using vitamin C (VC) as reducing agent. As shown in Figure S15, the vitamin C has no quenching effect on N-CQDs (Black line), while the quenching efficiency is further enhanced due to the reduction of quinone state material on the surface of N-CQDs after adding 50 μM VC into the CTC-N-CQDs system (Red line).

In addition, the fluorescence behavior of CQDs at pH 7.4 was also investigated (Figure S16). It is evident that CTC, RS and HQ have little influence on fluorescence intensity of CQDs at the excitation wavelength of 310 nm, 370 nm and 460 nm in the given concentration range, further demonstrating the crucial role of EDA connected on the N-CQDs in fluorescence response.

To elaborate the mechanism of fluorescence behavior originated from different emission sites, the “core state” fluorescence quenching was firstly investigated due to its relatively simple structure and excitation-independence. The corresponding λex of “core state” can be selected from Zone A. In this region, the emission peak width at λex of 310 nm is narrower than that at other lower excitation wavelength, which would be conducive to revealing the corresponding quenching trend. In the following experiments, the pH value was set at 7.4 due to the regular fluorescence quenching behavior under neutral condition, and 0.2 mg mL−1 was selected as the concentration of N-CQDs in the detection system based on the results shown in Figure S17.

According to the UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra analysis, the quenching of N-CQDs fluorescence is probable a static process originated from the Schiff base reaction or Michael addition reaction between CTC and EDA bonded on N-CQDs. To evaluate the “core state” quenching effect of N-CQDs in detail, various concentrations of CTC (from 0 to 1000 μM) were added into N-CQDs solution (0.2 mg mL−1). As shown in Fig. 6 a, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs at 525 nm decreases significantly with the increase of CTC concentration, and the quenching rate is more than 60 % when the CTC concentration reaches 100 μM. After that, the decrease trend of N-CQDs fluorescence gradually slows down with the addition of more CTC.

A further analysis was conducted using the Stern-Volmer model [52].
(1)
F
0
/
F
- 1
=
K
SV
Q
=
k q
τ
0
Q
where F 0 and F is the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs in the absence and presence of CTC, respectively; K SV is the Stern − Volmer quenching constant; [Q] is the concentration of CTC; k q is the quenching rate constant, and τ 0 is the fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs. As presented in Fig. 6b, K SV is calculated to be 0.00855 μM−1 according to the slope of regression line. Taking τ 0 (8.25 ns) and K SV into the above formula, k q is calculated to be 1.04 × 1012 M-1 s−1, which is much higher than the maximum value (1.0 × 1010) of dynamic quenching [52,53], also verifying dynamic quenching is highly unlikely to happen in the detection system of this study.

In addition, the possibility of inner filter effect (IFE) can also be excluded in N-CQDs system. This is because the emission wavelength of N-CQDs is above 500 nm at the excitation wavelength of 310 nm or 460 nm (Figure S4), and the absorptions of CTC, CTC-EDA complex and N-CQDs-CTC complex are all very weak in this wavelength range (Figure S10 and S12), which means no remarkable overlaps between the emission of N-CQDs and absorption of CTC or the produced static compounds. Therefore, the IFE may happen with a very low frequency [54]. The static chemical interaction between CTC and EDA is the possible extrinsic quenching mechanism.

As discussed previously, the covalent compounds on the surface of N-CQDs in reduced state might have a stronger fluorescence quenching ability than their oxidized state (the quinone state). Kar et al. reported that dopamine can transfer its own electrons to quantum dots and interfere with the recombination of excitonic [55]. Another work carried out by Mattoussi et al. found that dopamine and quinone (oxidized state of dopamine) can function as electron accepters to obtain the excited electrons from the conduction band of quantum dots and at the same time give electrons to the valence band (or holes) on the surface of quantum dots as electron donors. As dopamine was oxidized, more excited electrons were transferred from the conduction band to quinone, which shortened the fluorescence lifetime of exciton [56].

In this study, the fluorescence lifetime has not changed significantly after addition of CTC, which illustrates that the possibility of excited electrons transferring from LUMO (Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital) of N-CQDs to complex formed between CTC and EDA is very low. These static complexes are likely to play a major role as an electron donor in the fluorescence quenching process of N-CQDs. They might transfer their own electrons to the ground state (HOMO, Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital) of fluorescent groups distributed on the N-CQDs surface and hinder the excitonic recombination process. Therefore, it is not difficult to understand that the fluorescence quenching is weakened under alkaline conditions and enhanced in the presence of VC. The static complex on the surface of N-CQDs is easier to be oxidized at alkaline pH. The quinone (oxidized state) with higher oxidation potential has lower electron transfer ability towards the ground state (HOMO) of N-CQDs surface [56]. On the contrary, the fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs will be strengthened when VC is added as reducing agent. The surface fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-CQDs is shown in Fig. 8 .

In addition to the surface quenching mechanism, the quenching mechanism inside N-CQDs was further analyzed on the basis of the three emission states of N-CQDs in this study. As discussed previously, N-CQDs have three kinds of fluorescence emission states, i.e. “core state”, “edge state” and “surface state”. The fluorescence performances of N-CQDs at different pH and quenching agent (CTC) concentration show the surface state fluorescence (e.g. λex = 460 nm) is sensitive and easy to be quenched due to the direct exposure of surface state to solution. Although the “core state” (e.g. λex = 310 nm) is the innermost light-emitting state, it shows almost the same fluorescence response with the “surface state” when pH value or CTC concentration changes, which indicates some inherent connections exist between the “surface state” and “core state”. Wen et al. observed a fast trapping process of excited electrons from the sp2 nano domains into the “surface states” of CQDs [32]. In this study, the excitation wavelength independence of core state fluorescence illustrates a consistent source of excited electrons. At shorter excitation wavelength (e.g. λex = 310 nm), the electrons are mainly excited from the HOMO band of sp2 carbon core, then lose part of energy through relaxation and enter the corresponding LUMO band, which are likely to be trapped by the defects of “surface state”. The radiation inactivation in the “surface state” will produce fluorescence. Therefore, when CTC molecules were introduced onto the surface of N-CQDs, the fluorescence from “core state” is also quenched.

The “edge state” of N-CQDs shows its unique “inertness” to CTC concentrations or pH change. There might be a competition between the capture of excited electrons from “edge state” by the defects of “surface state” and radiative recombination in the “edge state”, because the sites of “surface state” and “edge state” are very close [32,57]. It is clear that the recombination of excited carriers in “edge state” plays a dominant role, so that the fluorescence intensity of “edge state” remains stability in this study. The intrinsic fluorescence quenching mechanisms of N-CQDs are shown in Fig. 9 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of N-CQDs for CTC detection
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the good liner relationship between “core state” fluorescence intensity quenching of N-CQDs and CTC concentrations, a platform for CTC detection could be established. According to Fig. 6, the detection limit was calculated to be 0.65 μM (0.07 mg L-1) using the equation: LOD = 3σ/S, where σ is the standard deviation of the signals (n = 11) and S is the slope of the linear calibration plot (σ = 0.00186, S =0.00855 μM-1). In addition, a comparison between the detection method established in this study and CTC analytical methods reported in other researches is shown in Table S1. It can be seen that this novel detection system has good analytical capability for CTC.

According to the fluorescence response time figure (Figure S18), the fluorescence intensity decreases sharply within 5 s when CTC is added to the N-CQDs detection system, indicating that the detection system has a rapid fluorescence response. Then the fluorescence intensity continues to decrease and reaches a stable level within 30 min, which begins to decreased slowly again after 10 h. Therefore, the detection time of this method should be controlled within 10 h. The effect of temperature on CTC detection was also investigated in this work. As shown in Figure S19, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs system with addition of CTC remains stable in the range of 0–50 ℃.

The selectivity is an important parameter of a detection system. Besides HQ and RS, the possible interferences from other organic pollutants (phenol, acetamiprid, benzoic acid, p-aminophenol, oxytetracycline, tryptophan, lysine, tyrosine) and ions (Ba2+, Ca2+, C2O7 2−, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, NH4+, SiO3 2−, Zn2+, Cl-, Na+, SO4 2−, NO3-, Co2+, Cr3+ and Al3+) were evaluated. As shown in Fig. 10 , all the chosen reagents have only slight effect on the fluorescence of N-CQDs. Furthermore, the anti-interference capacity of the CTC detection system by N-CQDs was also tested (Figure S20). The fluorescence quenching rates of N-CQDs systems remain basically stable when CTC exists alone or coexists with various organics and ions. EDTA (1 mL, 50 μM) was added when Fe2+ or Cu2+ coexisted with CTC, because these two ions can enhance the quenching rate caused by CTC, and EDTA can effectively eliminate these interferences [58]. In general, these results indicate the N-CQDs-based sensing system has high selectivity and strong anti-interference ability for CTC detection.

The applicability of N-CQDs in practical samples detection was evaluated using standard addition recovery method. As shown in Table S2, the recoveries of CTC are ranged from 93.36%–127.54%, and the relative standard deviations (RSD) are from 2.44 to 8.39 %. These results demonstrate the suitability and high reproducibility of the N-CQDs system established in this study for the determination of CTC in natural water samples.

In this work, we found CTC could chemically combine with EDA molecules connected on the surface of CQDs to form static compounds, resulting in a statistic fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs. The quenching mechanisms involves two aspects. On the surface of N-CQDs, the produced EDA-CTC compounds could transfer their electrons to surface emission sites and inhibit the radiation recombination of carriers. This quenching ability can be enhanced when EDA-CTC compounds are in reduction state. The excited electrons coming from sp2 carbon core are easily trapped by “surface state”, then the radiation deactivation of these electrons on surface sites are also hindered. Therefore, the fluorescence produced by “core state” and “surface state” are vulnerable to the external environment, while the “edge state” of N-CQDs exhibits “inertness” to CTC concentration and pH change due to the in situ radiation recombination of carriers. Under the excitation wavelength (λex = 310 nm) corresponding to core state, a facile fluorescence detection system based on N-CQDs was developed, which can rapidly, sensitively, and selectively determine CTC under the neutral condition.
The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.
Lei Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Jiaxin Wen: Data curation. Kunjian Li: Formal analysis. Lu Liu: Validation. Wei Wang: Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors of this manuscript are: Lei Yang, Jiaxin Wen, Kunjian Li, Lu Liu, Wei Wang. All authors have seen the manuscript and approved to submit to your journal. The authors claim that none of the material in the paper has been published or is under consideration for publication elsewhere.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Special S&T Project on Water Pollution Control and Treatment (2017ZX07107002), National Key Research and Development Program (2018YFC1802000), and the Natural Science Foundation of Tianjin (No. 19JCYBJC23400).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2021.129557.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A facile approach towards amino-coated polyethersulfone particles for the removal of toxins

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Song, R. Wang, W. Zhao, S. Sun, C. Zhao, A facile approach towards amino-coated polyethersulfone particles for the removal of toxins, J Colloid Interface Sci 485 (2017) 39-50.
H. Lee, N.F. Scherer, P.B. Messersmith, Single-molecule mechanics of mussel adhesion, Proceedings Of the National Academy Of Sciences Of the United States Of America 103 (2006) 12999-13003.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Two-step hydrothermal preparation of carbon dots for calcium ion detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Yue, L. Li, L. Cao, M. Zan, D. Yang, Z. Wang, Z. Chang, Q. Mei, P. Miao, W.F. Dong, Two-Step Hydrothermal Preparation of Carbon Dots for Calcium Ion Detection, ACS Appl Mater Interfaces 11 (2019) 44566-44572.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly selective and sensitive fluorescence detection of hydroquinone using novel silicon quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Liu, Q. Wang, S. Guo, P. Jia, Y. Shui, S. Yao, C. Huang, M. Zhang, L. Wang, Highly selective and sensitive fluorescence detection of hydroquinone using novel silicon quantum dots, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical 275 (2018) 415-421.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: On-off-on fluorescent carbon dot nanosensor for recognition of chromium(VI) and ascorbic acid based on the inner filter effect

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Zheng, Z. Xie, D. Qu, D. Li, P. Du, X. Jing, Z. Sun, On-off-on fluorescent carbon dot nanosensor for recognition of chromium(VI) and ascorbic acid based on the inner filter effect, ACS Appl Mater Interfaces 5 (2013) 13242-13247.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum dot-based OFF/ON probe for detection of glutathione

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Banerjee, S. Kar, J.M. Perez, S. Santra, Quantum Dot-Based OFF/ON Probe for Detection of Glutathione, Journal of Physical Chemistry C 113(2009) 9659-9663.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: On the pH-dependent quenching of quantum dot photoluminescence by redox active dopamine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Ji, G. Palui, T. Avellini, H.B. Na, C. Yi, K.L. Knappenberger, Jr., H. Mattoussi, On the pH-dependent quenching of quantum dot photoluminescence by redox active dopamine, J Am Chem Soc 134 (2012) 6006-6017.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Surface sensitive photoluminescence of carbon nanodots: coupling between the carbonyl group and pi-electron system

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Liu, L. Bao, M. Yang, S. Zhang, M. Zhou, B. Tang, B. Wang, Y. Liu, Z.L. Zhang, B. Zhang, D.W. Pang, Surface Sensitive Photoluminescence of Carbon Nanodots: Coupling between the Carbonyl Group and pi-Electron System, J Phys Chem Lett 10 (2019) 3621-3629.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorometric and colorimetric determination of hypochlorite using carbon nanodots doped with boron and nitrogen

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.X. Wang, X. Jin, Y.F. Gao, F.Y. Kong, W.J. Wang, W. Wang, Fluorometric and colorimetric determination of hypochlorite using carbon nanodots doped with boron and nitrogen, Mikrochim Acta 186 (2019) 328.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Lei Yang Acadmic Qualifications: (1) Masters by research, specialized subject: Environmental engineering. 2017/09–2020/06 College of Environmental Science and Technology, Nankai University, China. (2) Bachelor of education, specialized subject: Environmental engineering. 2013/09–2017/06 College of Earth and Environment, Anhui University of Science and Technology, China

Wei Wang obtained her pH.D.degree from Nankai University in 2008. She joined Nankai University in 2008. She is now an associated professor in the College of Environmental Science and Technology. Her research group focuses on development of environmental functional material as well as the related applications in environment.
